reason: IMPRECISE CLAIM: φ(γ(τ)) CALLED 'A SCALAR' — The proof lists 'φ(γ(τ)): a scalar' among the ingredients. This is misleading in the distributional context. A test function φ ∈ D(R^4) is a FIXED function on R^4; it does NOT transform under Lorentz transformations. When we define T' in the boosted frame using the transformed worldline γ'=Λγ, the pairing involves φ(γ'(τ)) = φ(Λγ(τ)), which is NOT the same as φ(γ(τ)). To relate <T', φ> to <T, ψ> for some ψ, one must set ψ = φ∘Λ (the pullback of φ by Λ). The statement that φ is 'a scalar' elides this crucial point: in the distributional transformation law, the test function changes (it gets pulled back by Λ), and it is the RELATIONSHIP φ(Λγ(τ)) = (φ∘Λ)(γ(τ)) that makes the calculation work. Calling φ 'a scalar' without this clarification turns the distributional proof into a circular physicist's argument where one simultaneously transforms the coordinates and the test function without saying so.
